keyword.primary: how many words do you need to pass the A2 inburgering exam
variants: how many Dutch words for A2
how many words to pass inburgering
Dutch vocabulary needed for inburgering exam
how many words to learn for A2 Dutch
keyword.searcher_intent: A learner, often with a deadline and limited time, who wants a target number so they know how big the job is. Underneath the number question is real anxiety: 'is this achievable in the time I have?'

audience.persona: Beginner with low vocabulary and a deadline (e.g. around 100 words known with the exam weeks away); full-time workers and parents across all motivation patterns
audience.pain_summary: They have heard you cannot pass without vocabulary and want to know how many words is enough. They may know only a hundred words with a deadline looming, and they need both a realistic target and a way to learn that does not mean drowning in flashcards they will forget.

angle: Answer the number honestly up front (readers came for it), then make the more useful point: the count matters far less than which words and whether you can actually produce them. A smaller set of high-frequency, daily-use words you can speak beats a large passive list you only recognize. This reframes a quantity question into a quality-and-usage question, which is where the reader's real risk lives, and it is exactly the gap that trips people up in the speaking and writing parts.
thesis_sentence: A2 needs roughly a low-thousands vocabulary, but the number is the least important part of the answer: passing depends on knowing the right high-frequency everyday words and being able to use them, not on the size of a list you can only recognize.

outline.notes: Give the number range immediately for AEO and reader trust, with an honest caveat that estimates vary and the exam does not test a fixed word count. Then pivot to the thesis: the count is a starting frame, not the goal. Open with the answer, no throat-clearing.
outline.evidence_needed: VERIFY the A2 vocabulary range against CEFR / named language-learning sources before publishing (commonly cited figures exist but must be checked and attributed). Do not state a precise number as fact without a source. Cap academic citations at 1-2.

outline.section: Why the number matters less than you think
outline.notes: The core reframe. Two learners with the same word count perform very differently depending on whether those words are high-frequency and whether they can produce them. Recognition (seeing a word and knowing it) is easier than production (recalling and using it), and the exam's speaking and writing parts test production. This connects vocabulary directly to the production gap.
outline.evidence_needed: Persona production-gap framing. Optional single academic anchor on production vs recognition (the Hopman & MacDonald reference in the knowledge base) used sparingly per the citation cap.

outline.notes: Practical and course-agnostic: prioritize high-frequency daily-life vocabulary (the words the exam scenarios actually use) over thematic breadth. Give categories or examples of daily-use domains. This is the 'daily usage words' insight made actionable, and it is useful no matter what tool the reader uses.
outline.evidence_needed: Anonymized community advice ('learn the daily usage words', 'set a daily word target'). Keep recommendations applicable to free resources, Anki, or any course, per the advice-section test.

outline.section: How to learn words so they stick (and so you can use them)
outline.notes: Method, not just targets. Learn words in context and sentences rather than isolated lists; test yourself by producing, not just recognizing; reuse words in speaking and writing. Weave the learn-by-using value prop as undercurrent here, and follow the specificity rule strictly: describe how OUR course is designed (a word counts when you have used it), not as a universal requirement everyone must meet.
outline.evidence_needed: Product mechanism: words learned in context, lesson completes on use-in-conversation. Phrase as 'Dutchwithjoost is designed so...' NOT 'effective learning requires...'. proof-library/product-design-evidence.md. NO pricing (MOFU).

outline.section: If you are short on time before the exam
outline.notes: Speak to the deadline reader directly with realistic, honest guidance: focus on the highest-frequency words and the parts where vocabulary pays off fastest, and be candid that cramming a passive list rarely survives the speaking part. Avoid false reassurance. Keep it genuinely helpful.
outline.evidence_needed: Community deadline case (anonymized, e.g. limited vocabulary weeks before the exam). Do not promise outcomes or timelines you cannot support.

outline.notes: Self-contained AEO answers: the number question, recognize-vs-use, how many words per day, whether flashcards are enough.
outline.evidence_needed: Reuse verified figures from the short-answer section; keep answers consistent with the body. No new unverified claims.

value_props_to_weave.pain: Memorized word lists that did not stick and could not be used in conversation
value_props_to_weave.prop: Learning words in context and using them means they stay with you and become usable
value_props_to_weave.proof: Co-founder testing: 'The words that you learn you use... it helps me communicate' (verify against proof-library)

value_props_to_weave.pain: A big passive vocabulary that still collapses in the speaking and writing parts
value_props_to_weave.prop: A completion standard based on using words turns recognition into production
value_props_to_weave.proof: Product mechanism: in our course a word counts once you have used it in conversation (state as our design, not a universal rule)

value_props_to_weave.pain: Overwhelmed by how many words there seem to be
value_props_to_weave.prop: Focusing on high-frequency daily words shrinks the job to what actually appears on the exam
value_props_to_weave.proof: Persona lexicon: learners who passed describe targeting 'daily usage words' rather than exhaustive lists

originality_nuggets: Reframe the most-Googled version of the question (a number) into the one that predicts passing (which words, and can you produce them); few competitors resist just quoting a figure.
Tie vocabulary size directly to the speaking-exam failure pattern: a passive list is the hidden reason a well-prepared reader still fails spreken.
Make the recognition-vs-production distinction concrete at the word level, which most 'how many words' articles never do.

seo.title_tag: How Many Words to Pass the A2 Inburgering Exam?
seo.meta_description: How many Dutch words do you need for the A2 inburgering exam? The honest answer, and why which words you learn matters far more than the count.

aeo_directives.featured_snippet_target: How many words do you need to pass the A2 inburgering exam?
aeo_directives.definition_paragraph: Reaching A2 Dutch generally means a working vocabulary in the low thousands of words, but the inburgering exam does not test a fixed count. What decides whether you pass is knowing the right high-frequency, everyday words and being able to use them in speech and writing, not the raw size of a list you can only recognize.
faq_questions: How many Dutch words do you need for A2?
Does the inburgering exam test a fixed number of words?
How many new Dutch words should I learn a day?
Are flashcards enough to pass the inburgering vocabulary?
